items.mistake: Letting pandas infer dtypes on large CSVs without inspection
items.fix: Pass a `dtype` dict and `parse_dates`; spot-check with `df.head()` and `df.dtypes` on a sample.
items.why: Inference uses a row sample—columns with leading zeros or mixed types later in the file can parse incorrectly.
items.example: A zipcode column inferred as float64 shows 501.0 instead of "00501"; reload with `dtype={"zipcode": "string"}`.

items.mistake: Loading all columns when the model uses a handful
items.fix: Use `usecols` (CSV) or `columns` (Parquet) to read only required fields.
items.why: Wide tables can be 10× larger in memory than the feature subset actually needed.
items.example: A 500-column warehouse table needs 12 features—`usecols=feature_list` avoids loading 488 unused columns.

items.mistake: Computing fillna mean or category levels on the full DataFrame right after load
items.fix: Split or index-partition first; fit imputers and vocabularies on train rows only, then apply to val/test.
items.why: Global statistics include holdout rows and leak target-related structure into training features.
items.example: `df["amount"].fillna(df["amount"].mean())` before split puts test-row amounts into the mean used on train rows.

items.mistake: Storing high-cardinality strings as object dtype
items.fix: Cast to pandas `string` or `category` when cardinality is modest; for free-text fields keep object but drop unused columns.
items.why: Object columns store Python pointers per cell—far heavier than category codes for repeated labels.
items.example: `df["country"] = df["country"].astype("category")` on 200 unique values in 2M rows saves hundreds of MB.
